finding: Focusing on body does not strip all mental capacities but redistributes them: reduces agency (self-control, planning) while increasing experience (feeling, sensation). Moral status shifts accordingly—less morally responsible but more deserving of protection from harm.

Experiential purchases integrate more tightly into self-concept and autobiographical narrative than possessions, independent of cost or objective significance, and this closeness to self mediates greater satisfaction over time.

Carrying important secrets activates embodied metaphor of physical burden: perceived hill steepness increases, distances seem farther, physical tasks appear more effortful, and willingness to help with physical tasks decreases—proportional to secret severity.

People diminish egocentric bias and improve relative accuracy in estimating others' knowledge when they recognize their own knowledge is biased by recent exposure, shifting from experience-based to theory-based judgment without slowing their estimates.

Incidental exposure to money increases endorsement of free-market systems, belief that victims deserve their fate, preference for group-based hierarchy, and willingness to support market allocation even when it disadvantages the poor—especially among Americans.

Overconfident individuals are perceived as more competent and attain higher status in groups because their biased self-perceptions drive behavioral displays (speaking more, confident tone, relaxed posture) that observers use to infer competence, even when actual ability is matched.
